objective: Students will be able to interpret and construct UML class diagrams to represent object-oriented software designs, including classes, attributes, methods, and relationships such as association, aggregation, composition, and inheritance.

subtopics.title: UML as a Visual Language of Interaction
subtopics.description: Introduces UML notation as a 'grammar of interaction,' emphasizing its role in translating abstract design ideas into structured visual representations. Uses real-world examples (e.g., GitHub repositories) to contextualize notation.
subtopics.teaching_approach: Retrieval practice with flashcards for notation; guided analysis of real diagrams; timed drills for notation accuracy.

subtopics.title: Class Relationships as Stories of Interaction
subtopics.description: Explores associations, aggregation, and composition through storytelling. Students create fictional scenarios (e.g., 'library system') to practice modeling relationships.
subtopics.teaching_approach: Comparison/contrasting cases; role-play activities; self-explanation prompts (e.g., 'How does this hierarchy simplify system complexity?').

subtopics.title: Inheritance and Polymorphism Through Analogies
subtopics.description: Defines inheritance and polymorphism using real-world analogies (e.g., biological taxonomies). Students identify patterns in existing diagrams and explain design choices.
subtopics.teaching_approach: Induction via pattern recognition; self-explanation prompts; concept mapping.

subtopics.title: Step-by-Step UML Construction with Real-World Problems
subtopics.description: Breaks down UML diagram creation into actionable steps using authentic problems (e.g., 'Design a UML diagram for a ride-sharing app’s user authentication module').
subtopics.teaching_approach: Structured practice with guided templates; interleaving exercises; timed drills for notation accuracy.
search_queries: UML diagram creation for real-world systems

subtopics.title: Interpreting UML Through Collaborative Inquiry
subtopics.description: Teaches learners to reverse-engineer diagrams by analyzing classes, relationships, and constraints. Focuses on *why* design choices matter (e.g., clarity for stakeholders).
subtopics.teaching_approach: Collaborative problem-solving; open-ended questions; peer review.

subtopics.title: Simplifying Complexity and Avoiding Pitfalls
subtopics.description: Highlights common errors (e.g., overloading diagrams) and strategies for clarity. Students redesign messy diagrams to align with aesthetic and functional best practices.
subtopics.teaching_approach: Collaborative discussion; analogical reasoning (e.g., comparing UML to blueprints or city maps).

subtopics.title: UML as a Tool for Creative Problem-Solving
subtopics.description: Synthesizes all elements into a capstone project: designing a UML diagram for a real-world scenario (e.g., a social media platform’s user management system).
subtopics.teaching_approach: Fluency-building through timed, open-ended tasks; peer and instructor feedback; reflection on design trade-offs.

subtopics.title: Design Aesthetics in UML Class Diagrams
subtopics.description: Teaches students to balance functionality and visual appeal in diagrams. Through case studies of open-source projects, students experiment with color, typography, and spatial arrangement.
subtopics.teaching_approach: Visual storytelling; peer critique; reflective journaling ('Does this diagram feel like a symphony or a maze?').

subtopics.title: Reverse-Engineering Real-World UML Diagrams
subtopics.description: Analyzes open-source UML diagrams to understand how professionals balance clarity, complexity, and aesthetics. Students critique and refine existing diagrams.
subtopics.teaching_approach: Case studies; group analysis; guided questions (e.g., 'Why was composition preferred over aggregation here?').
